title: Comments and Code Organization
description: Use comments to document your code and keep it tidy.

slides.title: Why Comments? 💬
slides.content:
Comments are notes you leave in your code to explain what it does. They are ignored by R when running the code. Good comments help:

* You understand your own code when you revisit it later.
* Others (collaborators, instructors) understand your logic.
* You debug by temporarily disabling parts of code.

In R, single-line comments start with the `#` symbol. Everything after `#` on that line is a comment.

slides.title: Single-Line Comments
slides.codeSnippet:
# This is a comment
x <- 10  # This is also a comment after code
y <- 20
# The line below calculates the sum
sum_xy <- x + y
print(sum_xy)

quizData.question: Which line contains a comment that will NOT affect the output?
quizData.explanation: In R, only lines starting with # (or after code with #) are comments. The others would cause errors because R doesn't understand the text without #.

slides.title: Multi-line Comments?
slides.content:
R does not have a dedicated multi-line comment syntax like `/* ... */` in other languages. However, you can:

* Put `#` at the beginning of each line.
* Use an if (FALSE) { ... } block (but code inside won't run, and it's a bit of a hack).

For readability, it's best to just use `#` on each line.

Example:

```r
# This is a long comment
# that spans multiple lines.
# Each line starts with #.
```

slides.title: Pro Tip: Commenting Out Code 🚀
slides.content:
When debugging, you can temporarily disable a line of code by adding `#` at the front. This is called 'commenting out'.

```r
x <- 10
# x <- 20   # This line won't run
y <- 30
```

This way you can test alternative code without deleting anything.

slides.title: Well-Commented Script Example
slides.content: Here's a script that calculates the area of a circle with good comments:
slides.codeSnippet:
# Calculate area of a circle
# Author: R Learner
# Date: 2025-03-17

radius <- 5  # circle radius in cm
pi_value <- 3.14159  # approximate pi
area <- pi_value * radius^2  # formula: pi * r^2
print(area)  # display result

slides.title: Add Comments to This Script ✍️
slides.content: Add meaningful comments to the following script. Use `#` to explain what each line does.
slides.codeSnippet:
# Your commented version:
a <- 7
b <- 3
c <- a / b
print(c)
